title: 7 Signs Your Dog Has Joint Pain — And What to Do About It
seoTitle: 7 Signs Your Dog Has Joint Pain — Vet Warning Signs (2026)
metaDescription: Is your dog stiff, limping, or struggling to stand? These 7 vet-identified signs indicate joint pain. Learn what to do — including the #1 simple fix.
author: GetPawsy Pet Care Team

content.text: Joint Pain in Dogs Is More Common Than You Think

Arthritis affects 25% of all dogs over age 7 and up to 80% of dogs over 8. Large breeds like Labradors, German Shepherds, and Golden Retrievers are even more susceptible. The problem? Dogs hide pain instinctively. By the time you notice obvious limping, the joint damage is already significant.

The most common early sign. If your dog takes a few steps to 'warm up' after resting, or hesitates before standing, joint inflammation is the likely cause. This is especially noticeable after long naps or first thing in the morning.
Sign #2: Reluctance to Jump or Climb Stairs
Dogs with joint pain avoid high-impact movements. If your dog used to jump on the couch and now hesitates, or pauses at the bottom of stairs, their joints are telling them to stop.

Frequent repositioning, restless sleep, or avoiding their usual sleeping spot can indicate discomfort. A dog that suddenly prefers the cold tile floor over their bed may be seeking relief from heat buildup in inflamed joints.
Sign #4: Limping or Favoring a Leg
Obvious limping is a late-stage sign — by this point, significant cartilage damage has likely occurred. Even subtle gait changes, like a slight head bob or shorter stride, can indicate joint pain.
If your once-energetic dog now prefers to lie down instead of play, don't assume it's just aging. Pain-related activity reduction is gradual and easy to miss.
Dogs instinctively lick painful areas. Persistent licking or chewing at hip, knee, or elbow joints is a strong indicator of underlying pain.
Pain makes dogs grumpy. If your normally patient dog snaps when touched or seems withdrawn, chronic discomfort may be the cause.
The #1 Simple Fix: Upgrade Their Sleep Surface

Vets consistently recommend orthopedic memory foam beds as the first line of defense. Proper sleep surfaces reduce joint pressure by up to 40%, improve morning mobility, and slow arthritis progression. Combined with joint supplements and appropriate exercise, an [orthopedic bed](/dog/orthopedic-dog-beds) is the single most impactful change you can make.
See our expert picks: [Best Orthopedic Dog Beds for Joint Support](/dog/orthopedic-dog-beds)

faq.question: At what age do dogs start getting joint pain?
faq.answer: Joint pain can start as early as age 1 in breeds predisposed to hip dysplasia (e.g., German Shepherds). Most dogs begin showing signs between ages 5–8, with 80% of dogs over 8 having some degree of arthritis.

faq.question: Can a dog bed help with joint pain?
faq.answer: Yes. Orthopedic memory foam beds distribute weight evenly, reducing pressure on inflamed joints by up to 40%. Vets recommend them as part of comprehensive arthritis management.

faq.question: Should I take my dog to the vet for joint pain?
faq.answer: Yes. A vet can diagnose the specific cause (arthritis, dysplasia, injury) and recommend appropriate treatment. Early intervention significantly slows joint deterioration.
